note: Seed recipe library, hand-written 2026-07-06. Grow it with scripts/generate-recipes.js — every entry must pass the same schema.

recipes.name: Herb-Roasted Whole Chicken with Root Vegetables
recipes.tagline: One bird, one pan, and the kind of Sunday smell that pulls everyone into the kitchen.

ingredients.item: parsnips or potatoes, cut in thick chunks

instructions: Pull the chicken out of the fridge an hour before cooking and pat it very dry. Salt it generously inside and out.
Heat the oven to 425°F. Toss the carrots, parsnips, and onions with a little oil, salt, and pepper, and spread them in a roasting pan or large cast-iron skillet.
Mash the butter with a spoonful of chopped thyme and rosemary. Work most of it under the breast skin and rub the rest over the bird.
Stuff the cavity with the garlic, lemon halves, and remaining herb sprigs. Tie the legs if you like. Set the bird on top of the vegetables.
Roast 70 to 85 minutes, until the breast reads 155–160°F and the thigh 175°F. If it browns too fast, tent loosely with foil.
Rest the bird 15 minutes on a board while you stir the vegetables through the pan drippings. Carve and serve everything from the pan.
Save the carcass in a freezer bag — it makes about 2 quarts of stock and that is not optional in this house.
recipes.marketTip: After the first frost, root vegetables get noticeably sweeter — October through winter carrots and parsnips are a different vegetable than the summer ones. Carrots sold with their tops still on are the freshest at the stand.
recipes.pvfNote: Our Cornish Cross birds live on fresh grass and a corn-and-soy-free ration from County Line Feeds. That's why the meat is darker and tastes like chicken used to — and why the drippings under those vegetables are worth fighting over.

recipes.name: Crispy Chicken Thighs with Sweet Corn and Cherry Tomatoes
recipes.tagline: Skin-side down until it crackles, then July sweet corn and burst tomatoes in the same pan.
instructions: Heat the oven to 425°F. Pat the thighs dry and season well with salt and pepper.
Set an oven-safe skillet over medium heat with the oil. Lay the thighs in skin-side down and leave them alone for 12 to 14 minutes, until the skin is deeply browned and releases on its own.
Flip the thighs and move them to a plate. Pour off all but a couple tablespoons of fat.
Add the corn, tomatoes, and garlic to the skillet with a pinch of salt and red pepper flakes. Stir for 2 minutes, until the tomatoes just start to split.
Nestle the thighs back in skin-side up, keeping the skin above the vegetables. Slide the skillet into the oven for 12 to 15 minutes, until the thighs read 175°F.
Tear the basil over the top and serve straight from the pan with good bread.
recipes.marketTip: WNY sweet corn arrives mid-July and it's legendary — buy it the day you cook it, and don't peel the husk back at the stand (it just dries the ear out for the next person). Heavy ears with sticky brown silk are the fresh ones.
recipes.pvfNote: Thighs from a bird that actually walked around have more muscle and more flavor, and the skin renders crisp instead of flabby. Pasture-raised and corn-and-soy-free is the whole reason this four-ingredient dinner works.

recipes.name: Grilled Spatchcock Chicken with Charred Summer Squash
recipes.tagline: The whole bird on the grill in under an hour, no flare-up drama — spatchcocked is the cheat code.

ingredients.item: zucchini or summer squash, halved lengthwise
paprika, garlic powder, and black pepper, mixed

instructions: Salt the bird all over, ideally a few hours ahead. Rub it with the spice mix and a little oil.
Set the grill up for two zones: hot on one side, low or off on the other.
Lay the chicken skin-side up on the cool side, legs pointed toward the heat. Close the lid and cook 35 to 45 minutes, until the thigh reads about 165°F.
Move the bird skin-side down over the hot side for 3 to 5 minutes to crisp and char the skin. Watch it — drippings mean flames.
Rest the chicken 10 minutes. While it rests, toss the squash and scallions with oil and salt, and grill over the hot side until striped and tender, about 3 minutes a side.
Squeeze the lemon over everything, carve, and eat outside.
recipes.marketTip: Zucchini and summer squash are everywhere from late June on — smaller squash (under 8 inches) are firmer and sweeter on the grill than the baseball bats.
recipes.pvfNote: We spatchcock these birds at the processor so you don't have to wrestle poultry shears on a Saturday. Same pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free Cornish Cross as our whole birds, just flat — which means even cooking and all-crisp skin on a grill.

recipes.name: Cider-Braised Chicken Leg Quarters with Apples and Onions
recipes.tagline: Fall in a Dutch oven — chicken braised in Finger Lakes cider until it's falling off the bone.
instructions: Heat the oven to 350°F. Season the leg quarters well with salt and pepper.
Brown them skin-side down in butter in a Dutch oven over medium-high heat, about 8 minutes, until deeply golden. Set aside.
Cook the onions in the same pot until soft, about 5 minutes. Add the apples for the last minute.
Stir in the cider, mustard, and herbs, scraping up the browned bits. Nestle the chicken back in, skin above the liquid.
Braise uncovered in the oven for 45 to 50 minutes, until the meat is nearly falling off the bone and the cider has reduced to a glaze-y sauce.
Finish with a splash of cider vinegar to wake it up. Serve over mashed potatoes or egg noodles.
recipes.marketTip: Real pressed cider shows up at WNY farm stands in September and it makes this dish — the pasteurized jug kind works, but fresh cider has an acidity that balances the richness. Grab extra apples that hold their shape: Crispin, Northern Spy, or Empire.
recipes.pvfNote: Leg quarters are the most underpriced cut on a pastured bird — all dark meat, built for braising, from chickens raised on grass and a corn-and-soy-free ration. This is the cut I tell people to start with.

recipes.name: Farmhouse Chicken Noodle Soup from Scratch
recipes.tagline: Real stock from real bones, drumstick meat, and egg noodles — the soup that fixes February.

ingredients.item: chicken stock bag (backs, necks, and bones)

instructions: Put the stock bag contents and the drumsticks in your biggest pot with the bay leaves, a teaspoon of peppercorns, and half the onion. Cover with cold water by two inches.
Bring just to a simmer — never a hard boil — and skim any foam. Simmer gently, partly covered, for 2 hours.
Pull out the drumsticks after the first 45 minutes, before they overcook. Strip the meat and set it aside; return the bones to the pot for the rest of the simmer.
Strain the stock into a clean pot and salt it until it tastes like soup, not water. You should have about 3 quarts.
Simmer the carrots, celery, and remaining onion in the stock for 10 minutes. Add the noodles and cook until tender, about 7 minutes more.
Stir in the drumstick meat and the herbs, check the salt, and serve hot. It's better tomorrow, if it survives that long.
recipes.marketTip: Winter farmers markets in Rochester carry storage carrots, onions, and celeriac all season — frost-sweetened storage carrots make noticeably sweeter soup than supermarket ones.
recipes.pvfNote: Stock from pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free bones gels solid in the fridge — that's the collagen the label promises and rarely delivers. One stock bag plus a pack of drumsticks makes a week of soup for less than takeout for two.

recipes.name: Chicken Milanese with Spring Greens
recipes.tagline: Crackly golden cutlets under a pile of lemony greens — spring dinner in twenty-five minutes.

ingredients.item: olive oil, lard, or tallow, for frying

instructions: Slice each breast horizontally into two thin cutlets, then pound between parchment to an even quarter inch. Season with salt and pepper.
Set up three shallow dishes: flour, beaten eggs, and breadcrumbs mixed with the parmesan.
Dredge each cutlet in flour, then egg, then press firmly into the crumbs on both sides.
Heat the fat in a wide skillet over medium-high until it shimmers. Fry the cutlets 2 to 3 minutes a side, until deep golden. Drain on a rack, not paper towels — the crust stays crisp.
Toss the greens and radishes with lemon juice, a little olive oil, and salt.
Pile the salad right on top of the hot cutlets and serve with lemon wedges.
recipes.marketTip: May and June are peak lettuce season in the Genesee Valley — spicy arugula and French breakfast radishes both show up at the Brighton Farmers Market and they're exactly what this dish wants.
recipes.pvfNote: Breast from a pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free bird actually stays juicy pounded thin — it hasn't been water-plumped. And the egg wash is our eggs doing what deep-orange yolks do: making the crust brown faster and richer.

recipes.name: Sticky Honey-Garlic Wings
recipes.tagline: Grill-crisped wings in a glaze that has everyone licking their fingers and counting who took more.

ingredients.item: honey (local if you can get it)

instructions: Pat the wings very dry and salt them. On the grill: cook over medium indirect heat, lid closed, turning every 10 minutes, about 35 minutes total. In the oven: toss with baking powder and roast at 425°F on a rack for 40 minutes, flipping once.
Meanwhile simmer the honey, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and vinegar in a small pan for 3 to 4 minutes, until it coats a spoon.
When the wings are crisp and cooked through, move them over direct heat (or under the broiler) for 2 minutes a side to char the edges.
Toss the hot wings in the glaze in a big bowl until every wing is sticky.
Shower with scallions and serve with plenty of napkins.
recipes.marketTip: Local honey vendors are at nearly every WNY market all season — a wildflower honey with some character beats clover here. Grab scallions in the same trip.
recipes.pvfNote: Wings from birds that ranged on pasture have real meat on them — these aren't the sad flat-and-drumette splits from the grocery cooler. Corn-and-soy-free feed means the fat renders clean and crisps instead of smoking up the grill.

recipes.name: Pan-Seared Pork Chops with Maple-Apple Pan Sauce
recipes.tagline: Thick chops, brown butter, September apples, and a slick of WNY maple — fall on a plate.

ingredients.item: bone-in pork chops, about 1 inch thick

instructions: Salt the chops 30 minutes ahead if you can. Pat dry and season with pepper.
Sear in a tablespoon of butter in a heavy skillet over medium-high, 4 to 5 minutes a side, until browned and the center reads 135–140°F. Rest on a plate.
Drop the heat to medium. Add the shallot and apples to the pan and cook 3 minutes, until the apples take some color.
Pour in the cider, scraping up the browned bits, and reduce by half. Stir in the maple syrup and mustard.
Kill the heat and swirl in the last two tablespoons of butter, plus any juices from the resting chops.
Spoon the apples and sauce over the chops. The carryover heat brings them to a perfect blush-pink 145°F.
recipes.marketTip: WNY maple producers sell at markets year-round — darker grades have more flavor and stand up to pork better than the light stuff. Apple-wise, pick something that holds its shape: Empire, Crispin, or Northern Spy.
recipes.pvfNote: Idaho Pasture Pigs put on real intramuscular fat living outside on pasture with a corn-and-soy-free ration. That's why these chops stay juicy at a true medium — don't cook the pink out of them.

recipes.name: Smoked Pork Chops with Braised Cabbage and Potatoes
recipes.tagline: The chops are already smoked — you're twenty minutes from a supper that tastes like it took all day.

ingredients.item: salt, pepper, grainy mustard for serving

instructions: Simmer the potatoes in salted water until just tender, about 12 minutes. Drain.
Meanwhile, melt the butter in a wide, deep skillet over medium heat. Cook the onion 3 minutes, then add the cabbage, caraway, and a good pinch of salt.
Add the stock, cover, and braise the cabbage 10 minutes, until silky. Stir in the vinegar and the potatoes.
Push everything aside and lay the chops in the pan. They're fully smoked — they just need 3 to 4 minutes a side to heat through and pick up some color.
Serve the chops over the cabbage and potatoes with grainy mustard on the table.
recipes.marketTip: Storage cabbage and potatoes are the workhorses of the Rochester winter markets — a head of cabbage keeps a month in the fridge, so buy it when you see it.
recipes.pvfNote: These are our regular pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free chops, brined and smoked at Timberline Meats. The smoking is real — no liquid smoke, no shortcuts — which is why the pan smells like a smokehouse in about ninety seconds.

recipes.name: Carnitas Tacos from a Butt Roast
recipes.tagline: Slow-simmered, then crisped in its own fat — one roast, a pile of tortillas, and taco night is handled.

ingredients.item: pork butt roast, cut in 2-inch chunks
white onion, half quartered, half diced for serving
cumin, plus 2 bay leaves and 1 tbsp dried oregano

instructions: Put the pork in a Dutch oven with the quartered onion, garlic, spices, and a tablespoon of salt. Squeeze the orange over and drop the halves in. Add water to barely cover.
Bring to a simmer, then cook uncovered at a lazy bubble for about 2.5 hours, until the meat shreds with a fork and the water has mostly cooked away.
Keep going: as the liquid disappears, the pork starts frying in its own rendered fat. Let the chunks crisp and brown, turning gently, about 15 minutes. (Or spread on a sheet pan and broil 5 minutes.)
Shred coarsely, keeping the crispy edges, and taste for salt.
Warm the tortillas and serve with diced onion, cilantro, lime wedges, and whatever salsa is in the fridge. In August, chopped fresh tomatoes with a little jalapeño is all the salsa you need.
recipes.marketTip: Summer through early fall, make the salsa from the market: fresh tomatoes, onion, and hot peppers all peak together in August in WNY. In winter, a jar of good salsa verde does the job.
recipes.pvfNote: Carnitas is where an Idaho Pasture Pig shows off — the roast carries enough clean fat to confit itself, no added lard needed. Pasture-raised and corn-and-soy-free means that fat is worth eating, and it's where all the flavor lives.

recipes.name: Low-and-Slow Spare Ribs with Sweet Corn
recipes.tagline: Three unhurried hours to bend-and-tear ribs, finished sticky, with July corn on the side.

ingredients.item: rub: brown sugar, paprika, salt, pepper, garlic powder

instructions: Peel the papery membrane off the bone side of the rack (grab a corner with a paper towel and pull). Coat the ribs all over with the rub.
Set the grill for indirect heat around 275°F (coals or burners on one side), or just use the oven at 275°F.

Cook the ribs bone-side down, away from the heat, lid closed, for about 3 hours — no peeking every ten minutes. They're ready when the meat has pulled back from the bone tips and the rack bends deeply when you lift one end.
Brush with barbecue sauce and give them 15 more minutes over the heat to set the glaze.
Throw the corn on the grill in its husk for the last 15 minutes, turning a few times. Shuck, butter, salt.
Rest the rack 10 minutes, cut between the bones, and accept the compliments.
recipes.marketTip: Mid-July through Labor Day is WNY sweet corn's window, and corn grilled in the husk the day it was picked needs nothing but butter. A dozen ears costs less than a bag of chips — get extra for tomorrow's salad.
recipes.pvfNote: Spare ribs from our Idaho Pasture Pigs carry more meat between the bones than commodity racks, and the fat renders instead of dripping out — pasture-raised on a corn-and-soy-free ration does that. Low heat and patience is the whole recipe.

recipes.name: Italian Sausage with Blistered Peppers and Onions
recipes.tagline: The fair-stand classic, done at home with September peppers — on a roll or over polenta.

ingredients.item: mild Italian sausage links (or hot — dealer's choice)
bell or Italian frying peppers, sliced in strips
sturdy rolls, or polenta for serving

instructions: Brown the sausages in a little oil in a wide skillet over medium heat, turning, about 8 minutes — they don't need to cook through yet. Set aside.
Add the rest of the oil, then the peppers and onions with a pinch of salt and oregano. Cook, stirring occasionally, 12 to 15 minutes, until soft with blistered edges.
Stir in the garlic for the last minute.
Slice the sausages thick on the bias, return them to the pan with any juices, and cook 5 more minutes until cooked through.
Finish with a splash of vinegar, scraping the pan. Pile onto rolls or spoon over soft polenta.
recipes.marketTip: Late August into September, WNY pepper growers bring the good stuff — long Italian frying peppers (Carmen, Jimmy Nardello) are sweeter and thinner-walled than bells and blister beautifully. Buy ugly ones; they taste the same.
recipes.pvfNote: Our sausage is made from our own Idaho Pasture Pigs — pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free — seasoned and linked at Timberline Meats with nothing you wouldn't keep in your own spice drawer. Read a grocery sausage label sometime and you'll see why that sentence matters.

recipes.name: Hot Italian Sausage and Kale Pasta
recipes.tagline: Frost-sweetened kale, spicy crumbled sausage, pecorino, and pasta water doing its quiet magic.

ingredients.item: hot Italian sausage links, casings removed
kale (lacinato or curly), stemmed and torn
grated pecorino or parmesan, plus more to serve

instructions: Get a big pot of well-salted water boiling and cook the pasta a minute shy of the box.
Meanwhile, brown the sausage in olive oil in a wide skillet over medium-high, breaking it into rough crumbles, about 6 minutes. Don't rush the browning — that's the sauce.
Add the garlic, then the kale in big handfuls with a splash of pasta water. Cook until the kale is silky, about 4 minutes.
Scoop a cup of pasta water, then drain the pasta into the skillet.
Toss hard over the heat with the cheese and enough pasta water to make everything glossy. Finish with lemon zest, a squeeze of juice, and black pepper.
Serve with more cheese on top, obviously.
recipes.marketTip: Kale after the first frost is a different vegetable — sweeter, more tender. October through the winter markets, look for lacinato (the dark bumpy one). It's also one of the last things standing at WNY markets in December.
recipes.pvfNote: The heat in our hot Italian sausage is honest chili and spice, not mystery 'flavorings' — made from our pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free pigs at Timberline Meats. It renders clean fat that becomes the backbone of this sauce.

recipes.name: Chorizo and Egg Breakfast Tacos
recipes.tagline: Crumbled chorizo, soft-scrambled orange-yolk eggs, warm tortillas — weekend breakfast with some swagger.

ingredients.item: crumbled queso fresco or shredded cheddar

instructions: Brown the chorizo in a dry skillet over medium heat, breaking it up, 6 to 8 minutes, until crisp at the edges. Push it to one side.
Beat the eggs with a pinch of salt. Drop the heat to medium-low and pour the eggs into the open side of the pan, right into a little of that chorizo fat.
Scramble slowly, folding, until just barely set — they'll finish in the tortilla. Fold the chorizo in at the end.
Warm the tortillas directly over a burner flame or in a dry pan until puffed and spotted.
Build: eggs and chorizo, cheese, avocado, cilantro, lime, hot sauce. Eat standing at the counter like you mean it.
recipes.marketTip: In summer, skip the avocado and spoon on chopped fresh market tomatoes with a pinch of salt — August tomatoes and chorizo are made for each other.
recipes.pvfNote: Our chorizo comes from our Idaho Pasture Pigs, and the eggs from hens on the same pasture eating a corn-and-soy-free layer ration from County Line Feeds. Those deep-orange yolks aren't a filter. That's just what eggs look like when the hen eats right.

recipes.name: The Full Farm Breakfast
recipes.tagline: Breakfast sausage, crispy home fries, and fried eggs with yolks like the sun — the Saturday standard.

ingredients.item: toast and jam for the table

instructions: Start the potatoes: get them sizzling in butter in a wide skillet over medium heat with a pinch of salt. Stir every few minutes, not constantly — crust needs contact time. About 20 minutes total; add the onion halfway.
Meanwhile, brown the sausage links in a second pan over medium heat, turning, 10 to 12 minutes, until cooked through. Keep a spoonful of the fat.
Season the home fries with paprika, salt, and pepper when they're deeply browned.
Fry the eggs in the sausage fat: crack them in over medium, baste or flip to taste. Salt them in the pan.
Get everything onto warm plates at the same time — that's the only hard part of this recipe.
recipes.marketTip: New potatoes from July markets make the creamiest home fries; storage russets from winter markets make the crispiest. There's no wrong season for this breakfast.
recipes.pvfNote: The sausage is our own pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free pork in a simple breakfast seasoning, and the eggs come from hens that moved to fresh grass the morning they were laid... give or take. Weekend breakfast is the easiest place to taste what the feed changes.

recipes.name: The August BLT
recipes.tagline: Thick traditional bacon, a tomato that was on the vine yesterday, and mayo on good toast. That's it. That's the sandwich.

ingredients.item: big ripe slicing tomatoes (Brandywine if you find them)

instructions: Lay the bacon in a cold skillet, then bring it up to medium. Cook, turning occasionally, until it's exactly how you like it. Save the fat — that's tomorrow's home fries.
Slice the tomatoes thick. Salt them on a plate and let them sit five minutes — this is the step everyone skips and shouldn't.
Toast the bread. Mayo on both slices, edge to edge. This is not the place for restraint.
Build: lettuce on the bottom to guard the toast, then tomato, plenty of black pepper, then bacon.
Cut on the diagonal (it tastes better, don't ask why) and eat over the sink or at a table you don't mind dripping on.
recipes.marketTip: This sandwich exists for maybe six weeks a year, when WNY field tomatoes hit the markets in late July. Buy the heavy, ugly, deeply colored ones and never refrigerate them.
recipes.pvfNote: Traditional bacon from our Idaho Pasture Pigs, cured and smoked at Timberline Meats. Pigs that live outside on a corn-and-soy-free ration grow bacon with real depth — and it doesn't shrivel to half its size in the pan, because it isn't pumped with water.

recipes.name: Smoked Ham Hock and White Bean Soup
recipes.tagline: Two smoked hocks, a pound of beans, and a slow afternoon — the most supper for the fewest dollars all winter.

ingredients.item: dried white beans (great northern or cannellini), soaked overnight
kale or collards, torn (optional, very good)

instructions: Put the hocks, drained beans, and bay leaves in a big pot. Cover with water by two inches, bring to a simmer, and skim any foam.
Simmer gently, partly covered, for 2 hours, until the beans are creamy and the hock meat is loose on the bone. Top up with water if it drops below the beans.
Fish out the hocks. When cool enough to handle, pull off every scrap of meat and skin-side gelatin, chop, and return it to the pot. Discard the bones.
Add the carrots, onion, celery, and garlic. Simmer 20 more minutes, until tender. Stir in the kale for the last 5.
Season last — the hocks bring their own salt. Finish with a splash of cider vinegar and plenty of black pepper.
Serve with buttered bread. This soup is better on day two and it freezes like a champion.
recipes.marketTip: Winter storage vegetables — carrots, onions, celeriac — carry the Rochester winter markets, and celeriac is the sleeper here: it does everything celery does with twice the flavor.
recipes.pvfNote: Hocks are the cut most people skip and shouldn't. Ours come from pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free Idaho Pasture Pigs and get real smoke at Timberline Meats — two hocks season and thicken a whole pot the way no bouillon cube ever will.

recipes.name: Ginger Pork Lettuce Wraps
recipes.tagline: Hot, gingery ground pork piled into cold summer lettuce — dinner that feels like a snack in the best way.

ingredients.item: butter or bibb lettuce, leaves separated
chili crisp or sriracha, roasted peanuts (optional)

instructions: Stir together the soy sauce, honey, and vinegar.
Get a wide skillet properly hot. Add the pork and press it flat — let it sear untouched for 3 minutes so it browns instead of steams.
Break it up, add the ginger and garlic, and cook 4 to 5 minutes more, until cooked through with crispy bits.
Pour in the sauce and let it bubble and glaze the meat, about 2 minutes. Stir in most of the scallions.
Serve the skillet next to a platter of lettuce cups, cucumbers, remaining scallions, and chili crisp. Everyone builds their own — spoon, wrap, eat, repeat.
recipes.marketTip: Soft-head lettuces (butter, bibb) show up at WNY markets from late May and make the best cups — the leaves are little bowls. Market cucumbers in July snap like nothing from a plastic sleeve.
recipes.pvfNote: Ground pork from our Idaho Pasture Pigs is a whole-animal grind, not trimmings — pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free, and it browns with a depth that makes a five-ingredient sauce taste like takeout you'd drive for.

recipes.name: Herb-Butter Roasted Turkey Breast
recipes.tagline: Thanksgiving for six without the 5 a.m. alarm — a bone-in breast, herb butter, and ninety honest minutes.

ingredients.item: fresh sage, thyme, and rosemary, chopped

instructions: Salt the breast all over the night before if you can — even a few hours makes a difference. Bring it toward room temperature before roasting.
Heat the oven to 400°F. Mash the butter with the herbs, a teaspoon of salt, and plenty of pepper.
Work half the herb butter under the skin, directly onto the meat. Rub the rest over the outside.
Scatter the vegetables in a roasting pan, pour in the stock, and set the breast on top, skin up.
Roast 20 minutes at 400°F, then drop to 350°F and roast 60 to 75 minutes more, until the thickest part reads 155°F. Tent with foil if the skin is browning too fast.
Rest 20 minutes under loose foil (it climbs to a safe 165°F). Carve each lobe off the bone in one piece, then slice crosswise. Serve over the pan vegetables with the drippings spooned over.
recipes.marketTip: November markets still carry Brussels sprouts, sweet potatoes, and fresh herbs — sprouts sold on the stalk stay fresher and roast sweeter after frost.
recipes.pvfNote: Our turkeys range on the same Genesee Valley pasture as the chickens, on a corn-and-soy-free ration. A pastured bone-in breast has actual turkey flavor — this is the answer for holidays where a whole bird is too much and dry white meat isn't an option.

recipes.name: Cider-Braised Turkey Legs with Root Vegetables
recipes.tagline: The fairground favorite, grown up — turkey legs braised until the meat slips off the bone.
instructions: Heat the oven to 325°F. Season the legs generously and brown them hard in fat in a Dutch oven, about 5 minutes a side. Set aside.
Cook the onions in the same pot for 3 minutes, then add the cider, stock, thyme, and bay, scraping up the fond.
Return the legs, cover, and braise in the oven for 1.5 hours.
Add the carrots and parsnips around the legs and braise uncovered 40 more minutes, until the vegetables are tender and the meat is pulling from the bone.
Move the legs and vegetables to a platter and boil the braising liquid down by half into a glossy sauce. Season, pour over, and bring the whole pot to the table.
recipes.marketTip: This is a late-October dish: fresh cider, frost-sweet parsnips, and storage carrots all peak together at WNY stands right as the weather turns.
recipes.pvfNote: Legs from a turkey that actually walked its pasture are all working muscle — pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free, and built for exactly this kind of slow cooking. Rich as duck confit, at a fraction of the price per pound.

recipes.name: Red Wine Braised Lamb Shanks
recipes.tagline: Shanks braised until they wave a white flag, over something creamy — the special-occasion pot that cooks itself.

ingredients.item: salt, pepper, olive oil; polenta or mashed potatoes for serving

instructions: Heat the oven to 300°F. Season the shanks well and brown them on all sides in olive oil in a heavy Dutch oven — take your time, 10 to 12 minutes total. Set aside.
Cook the onion and carrots in the same pot for 5 minutes. Add the garlic for one more.
Pour in the wine and reduce by half, scraping the bottom. Add the tomatoes, stock, herbs, and orange zest.
Return the shanks — they should be mostly submerged. Cover and braise in the oven for 2.5 to 3 hours, turning once, until the meat is completely tender and the bone wiggles free.
Rest the shanks while you skim the fat and reduce the sauce until it coats a spoon. Taste for salt.
Serve a shank per person over polenta or mash, drowned in sauce. Open a second bottle of whatever went in the pot.
recipes.marketTip: Winter carrots and storage onions from the Rochester winter markets are all the fresh produce this needs — spend the rest of the budget on a wine you'd actually drink.
recipes.pvfNote: Our Katahdin and White Dorper lambs stay with their mothers until weaning and finish entirely on Genesee Valley pasture — no grain at all. Grass-fed lamb from hair sheep is mild and clean-tasting, and the shank is its best-kept secret.

recipes.name: Grilled Lamb Loin Chops with Mint and Spring Peas
recipes.tagline: Little lamb T-bones over hot coals, with peas and mint doing what peas and mint have always done.
instructions: Rub the chops with olive oil, the garlic, salt, and pepper. Let them sit at room temperature for 20 minutes while the grill gets properly hot.
Blanch the peas in salted boiling water for 90 seconds, then shock in cold water. Toss with sliced spring onions, torn mint, lemon juice, olive oil, and salt.
Grill the chops over direct high heat, 3 to 4 minutes a side for medium-rare (about 130°F). They're small — they move fast.
Rest 5 minutes under foil.
Pile the pea salad on a platter, arrange the chops on top, and squeeze the other half of the lemon over everything.
recipes.marketTip: Snap peas hit WNY markets in late May and shelling peas in June — buy more than the recipe needs, because half of them won't survive the ride home. Mint is at every herb stand all spring.
recipes.pvfNote: Loin chops are the tenderloin and strip of the lamb in one small cut. Ours come from Katahdin and White Dorper lambs raised with their mothers on pasture, never fed grain — mild enough for people who think they don't like lamb.

recipes.name: Asparagus and Spring Onion Frittata
recipes.tagline: May asparagus, sharp cheddar, and eggs with yolks that turn the whole thing gold.

ingredients.item: grated sharp cheddar or crumbled goat cheese

instructions: Heat the oven to 375°F. Whisk the eggs with the cream, a teaspoon of salt, plenty of pepper, and half the cheese.
Melt the butter in a 10-inch oven-safe skillet over medium heat. Cook the asparagus with a pinch of salt for 3 to 4 minutes, until bright and just tender. Add the spring onions for the last minute.
Pour in the eggs, stir once to distribute, and cook undisturbed for 2 minutes, until the edges just set.
Scatter the remaining cheese on top and slide the skillet into the oven for 10 to 12 minutes, until puffed and barely set in the center.
Shower with dill, cut into wedges, and serve warm or room temperature — it's just as good packed up for a Letchworth picnic.
recipes.marketTip: Asparagus is the star of May in WNY — thin, bright spears with tight tips, ideally picked that morning. It's a six-week season. When it's gone, it's gone.
recipes.pvfNote: A frittata is mostly eggs, so the eggs have to carry it. Ours come from hens on fresh Genesee Valley pasture eating a corn-and-soy-free layer ration from County Line Feeds — the yolks are deep orange and the difference shows in every slice.

recipes.name: Garlic-Butter Roasted Bone-In Chicken Breasts
recipes.tagline: The bone keeps it juicy, the butter bastes itself, and the pan sauce is four minutes of glory.
instructions: Heat the oven to 425°F. Salt the breasts well, ideally an hour ahead. Mash the butter with the garlic, thyme, and a pinch of salt.
Work half the garlic butter under the skin of each breast and rub a little over the top.
Toss the potatoes with oil and salt in an oven-safe skillet, set the breasts on top skin-side up, and roast 35 to 40 minutes, until the thickest part reads 155°F.
Rest the chicken on a board. Set the skillet over medium heat, add the greens, and wilt them in the pan drippings.
Push the greens aside, add the stock, and scrape up the browned bits. Kill the heat and swirl in the remaining garlic butter and a squeeze of lemon.
Slice the breasts off the bone, slice crosswise, and serve over the potatoes and greens with the pan sauce.
recipes.marketTip: Winter markets in Rochester carry storage potatoes and greenhouse or frost-hardy greens right through the cold months — kale that's been through a frost is sweeter than anything from September.
recipes.pvfNote: A bone-in breast from a pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free bird is a different cut than the water-plumped boneless kind — the bone and skin protect it, so you get white meat that actually tastes like the dark.

recipes.name: Canadian Bacon Breakfast Stacks
recipes.tagline: Toasted English muffin, seared Canadian bacon, a runny-yolk egg — the diner classic, done right at home.

ingredients.item: Dijon mustard, stirred into 1/4 cup mayo (cheater's sauce)

instructions: Toast the muffins and spread with the Dijon-mayo.
Sear the Canadian bacon in a dry skillet over medium-high, about 90 seconds a side, until the edges brown and curl. Pile three slices on each muffin bottom.
Fry the eggs in butter in the same pan — sunny or basted, but leave the yolk runny. That's the sauce.
Stack an egg on each, scatter with scallions, salt and pepper, cap with the muffin top or eat it open-faced.
Serve with hot sauce and napkins. Total time is under the length of one cartoon episode.
recipes.marketTip: This one's a year-rounder — but in June, a pile of market strawberries on the side turns it into a proper weekend breakfast.
recipes.pvfNote: Canadian bacon is the pork loin, brined and smoked at Timberline Meats — from the same pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free Idaho Pasture Pigs as everything else we sell. Lean, meaty, and nothing like the pressed discs on a fast-food sandwich.

recipes.name: Cider-Braised Fresh Ham Hocks with Cabbage
recipes.tagline: The unsmoked hock, braised until the meat surrenders — a beer-hall supper from a five-dollar cut.

ingredients.item: salt, pepper, grainy mustard for the table

instructions: Heat the oven to 300°F. Season the hocks and brown them on all sides in a little fat in a Dutch oven, about 10 minutes total.
Add the onions, cider, stock, caraway, and bay. The liquid should come halfway up the hocks.
Cover and braise in the oven for 2.5 hours, turning the hocks once, until the meat is loose on the bone.
Tuck the potatoes and cabbage wedges around the hocks, salt them, and braise uncovered 30 to 40 minutes more, until tender.
For crackling skin, run the pot under the broiler for 3 or 4 minutes at the end.
Serve a hock per person with the vegetables, a ladle of broth, and mustard. Napkins, not forks, do the last part.
recipes.marketTip: Storage cabbage, onions, and potatoes from the winter markets are all this needs — the cider is the WNY touch and worth seeking out over beer.
recipes.pvfNote: Fresh hocks are the same cut as our smoked ones, straight from the pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free pigs with no cure at all — which means all that skin and collagen turns silky in the braise. Cheapest Sunday dinner on the price list.

recipes.name: Maple-Glazed Ham Steak with Fried Eggs
recipes.tagline: A smoked ham steak seared in maple and butter, with eggs fried in the glaze. Breakfast for dinner is legal.

ingredients.item: black pepper; toast for the table

instructions: Pat the ham steaks dry. Sear in butter in a big skillet over medium-high, 3 minutes a side, until browned at the edges. They're fully cooked — you're building color and warming them through.
Drop the heat to low. Add the maple, mustard, and a splash of vinegar, and turn the steaks to glaze both sides, about 2 minutes.
Move the ham to plates and tent with foil. Crack the eggs straight into the sticky pan and fry them in what's left of the glaze.
Serve a half steak and two eggs per person with plenty of black pepper and toast for the yolk.
recipes.marketTip: WNY maple producers are at the Rochester markets year-round, and this is where the dark, late-season syrup shines — more maple flavor, stands up to smoke and salt.
recipes.pvfNote: Ham steaks come from the hind leg of our Idaho Pasture Pigs, brined and smoked at Timberline Meats. Pasture-raised and corn-and-soy-free means it's ham that tastes like pork, not just salt — one steak feeds two people easy.

recipes.name: The Full Irish (Rasher) Breakfast
recipes.tagline: Back bacon like they cut it in Cork — meaty, not crispy — with eggs, beans, and grilled tomato.

ingredients.item: tomatoes, halved (in summer, from the market)
white beans in tomato sauce (or good baked beans)

instructions: Warm the beans in a small pot on the back burner.
Fry the rashers in a big skillet over medium heat, 2 to 3 minutes a side. Irish bacon is back bacon — cook it like ham, tender and browned, not shattering-crisp. Keep warm.
In the bacon fat, brown the mushrooms hard, then sear the tomato halves cut-side down until blistered. Salt both.
Fry the eggs in butter, sunny side up.
Load the plates: rashers, eggs, tomato, mushrooms, beans, toast. Strong tea is regulation but coffee won't get you disqualified.
recipes.marketTip: In August, use real market tomatoes and this breakfast levels up dramatically. The rest of the year, halved cherry tomatoes blistered in the pan beat sad winter slicers.
recipes.pvfNote: Irish bacon is cut from the loin, like Canadian bacon but with the fat cap left on — brined and smoked at Timberline from our pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free pigs. That fat cap is the whole point. Don't trim it.

recipes.name: Jowl Bacon Carbonara
recipes.tagline: Jowl is what Roman carbonara actually calls for — crisp cubes, orange-yolk eggs, pecorino, no cream, no apologies.

ingredients.item: finely grated pecorino romano, plus more to serve

instructions: Whisk the eggs, yolks, cheese, and pepper in a big serving bowl until it looks like wet sand.
Render the jowl batons in a skillet over medium heat until deeply golden and crisp, 8 to 10 minutes. Keep the fat.
Cook the pasta in well-salted water to just shy of al dente. Steal a full cup of pasta water before draining.
Working fast: drain the pasta, toss it in the skillet with the jowl and fat for 30 seconds, then dump everything into the egg bowl OFF the heat.
Toss hard, adding pasta water a splash at a time, until the sauce turns glossy and clings. The residual heat cooks the egg into silk — direct heat scrambles it, so stay off the stove.
Serve immediately with more pecorino and pepper. Carbonara waits for no one.
recipes.marketTip: Nothing to buy at the market for this one — which is exactly why it's the dinner for the first week of March when the stands are thin and you need something glorious from the pantry and freezer.
recipes.pvfNote: Jowl bacon is our answer to guanciale — the cheek, cured and smoked at Timberline, from pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free Idaho Pasture Pigs. It renders softer and richer than belly bacon, and with yolks this orange the sauce comes out gold.

recipes.name: Pork Belly Ramen with Real Bone Broth
recipes.tagline: A lazy Sunday broth from real pork bones, crowned with seared belly and a soft egg — takeout can't touch it.

ingredients.item: garlic, halved, plus a knob of ginger
soy sauce or tamari, plus 1 tbsp miso if you have it

instructions: Roast the bones at 425°F for 30 minutes, then simmer them in a big pot of water with the garlic and ginger, partly covered, for 3 to 4 hours. Top up water as needed. You want 2 quarts of rich broth.
Meanwhile, salt the belly and roast it at 300°F for 2 hours, then rest, chill if you have time, and slice thick.
Soft-boil the eggs: 7 minutes in boiling water, straight into ice water, peel, and (if you're organized) marinate in a splash of soy while the broth finishes.
Strain the broth and season it with the soy and miso until it tastes like something you'd order twice.
Sear the belly slices hard in a dry skillet until the edges crackle.
Cook the noodles, build the bowls — noodles, broth, belly, half an egg, carrots, scallions, chili crisp — and slurp loudly. It's respectful.
recipes.marketTip: Winter carrots and storage scallions cover the vegetable end; this is a January project meal for the week the Rochester market is more root cellar than salad bar.
recipes.pvfNote: Bones from pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free pigs make broth that sets solid in the fridge — that's the collagen. At $3.25 a pound they're the best-kept secret on the price list, and the belly is where an Idaho Pasture Pig shows off its clean, sweet fat.

recipes.name: Backyard Hot Dog Night, Done Properly
recipes.tagline: Uncured pork dogs with snap, charred over real fire, and a topping bar that ends the ketchup argument peacefully.

ingredients.item: cucumbers, quick-pickled (sliced thin + vinegar + salt + 20 minutes)
cherry tomatoes, chopped with a little jalapeño (summer relish)
mustard (several), shredded cheddar, chili if you're ambitious

instructions: Make the quick pickles and the tomato relish first; they get better while you grill.
Grill the dogs over medium direct heat, rolling every minute or so, 6 to 8 minutes, until blistered and browned with a little char at the ends. Score them lightly first if you like the edges crispy.
Toast the buttered buns cut-side down on the grill for the last minute. This step is not optional.
Set out the topping bar and let everyone build their own. House rule: nobody comments on anybody else's dog.
Extra dogs keep in the fridge and make an elite next-day lunch, split and seared in a pan.
recipes.marketTip: July and August hand you the whole topping bar — sweet onions, cucumbers for quick pickles, and tomatoes for relish, all from one pass through the Brighton market.
recipes.pvfNote: These are whole-muscle pork dogs in natural casings, no nitrates or nitrites added, made at Timberline from our pasture-raised, corn-and-soy-free pigs. Read the ingredient list on a grocery pack of dogs sometime — then read ours.

recipes.name: Grilled Spatchcock Turkey
recipes.tagline: The whole holiday bird, flat on the grill, done in under two hours with smoke the oven can't fake — and the oven stays free for pies.

ingredients.item: butter, melted, with chopped sage and thyme
hardwood chunks or chips if you have them

instructions: One to two days ahead: pat the bird dry and salt it everywhere, including under the skin on the breast. Leave it uncovered in the fridge — this is the whole secret to juicy and crisp.
Set the grill for indirect heat around 350°F: coals or burners on the sides, a drip pan in the middle. Add wood for smoke if you have it.
Brush the bird with the herb butter, dust with the pepper and paprika, and lay it skin-side up over the drip pan, legs toward the hotter zones.
Lid closed, cook about 6 to 7 minutes per pound — start checking at 90 minutes. Pull at 155°F in the breast, 175°F in the thigh.
Rest 30 minutes under loose foil (it coasts up to temperature). Squeeze the grilled lemons over the carved meat.
Carve like a giant chicken: legs off, breasts off the bone in one piece, slice crosswise. Accept that the wings disappear before dinner.
recipes.marketTip: November markets still have fresh sage and thyme — grab Brussels sprouts and sweet potatoes for the oven space you just liberated.
recipes.pvfNote: Our seasonal turkeys range the same Genesee Valley pasture as the chickens, on a corn-and-soy-free ration, and we spatchcock them at HLW Acres so the hard part's done. A flat bird cooks evenly in half the time — no more gray breast waiting on pink thighs.

recipes.name: Ground Turkey Chili for a Crowd
recipes.tagline: A big honest pot of chili that simmers while the game's on — and somehow better the second day.

ingredients.item: peppers, diced (late-summer market peppers if you froze some)
chili powder, plus 1 tbsp cumin and 1 tsp smoked paprika
salt; cheddar, sour cream, scallions to serve

instructions: Get a Dutch oven hot with a little fat. Brown the turkey hard in two batches, pressing it flat and leaving it alone so it crusts — pale turkey makes pale chili. Set aside.
Cook the onion and peppers in the same pot until soft, 5 minutes. Add the garlic and all the spices and stir 1 minute, until it smells like a chili stand.
Return the turkey with the tomatoes, beans, and stock. Scrape the bottom.
Simmer, partly covered, 45 minutes to an hour, stirring now and then, until thick enough to stand a spoon in. Salt to taste.
Serve with the fixings bar. It freezes perfectly and reheats better than it started.
recipes.marketTip: September peppers from the market, diced and frozen flat in a bag, are the trick — you'll cook out of that bag all winter.
recipes.pvfNote: Ground turkey from a pastured, corn-and-soy-free bird is a whole different ingredient than the pale tubes at the grocery store — it browns like beef and holds its own against three tablespoons of chili powder.

recipes.name: Real Turkey Stock and the Day-After Soup
recipes.tagline: Necks and bones become two quarts of liquid gold, then the soup that outshines the feast that made it.

ingredients.item: egg noodles, rice, or wild rice
bay leaves, plus a teaspoon of peppercorns

instructions: Roast the stock-bag bones and necks at 425°F for 30 minutes, until browned — this is the difference between stock and dishwater.
Simmer everything in a big pot with one quartered onion, the bay, and peppercorns, covered by two inches of water, for 3 hours at a lazy bubble. Skim early, never boil hard.
Pull the necks after 2 hours and pick them — there's a shocking amount of sweet dark meat on a turkey neck. Set the meat aside.
Strain, salt the stock properly, and either jar it for the freezer or keep going:
Simmer the carrots, celery, and remaining onion in the stock 10 minutes, add the noodles or rice until tender, then stir in the neck meat and herbs.
Eat one bowl standing at the stove. Tradition.
recipes.marketTip: Celeriac from the winter markets does celery's job with twice the flavor and keeps for months in the crisper — buy one whenever you see it.
recipes.pvfNote: Bones and necks from pastured, corn-and-soy-free turkeys make stock that gels like it's supposed to. A stock bag and a pack of necks cost less than two boxes of the cartoned stuff and taste like actual turkey.

recipes.name: Turkey Thigh Cacciatore
recipes.tagline: Hunter's-style braise — turkey thighs, tomatoes, peppers, and mushrooms — over polenta on a cold night.

ingredients.item: garlic, sliced, plus 1 tsp dried oregano and a rosemary sprig
salt, pepper, olive oil; polenta or crusty bread to serve

instructions: Heat the oven to 325°F. Season the thighs and brown them skin-side down in olive oil in a Dutch oven, about 6 minutes. Set aside.
Brown the mushrooms hard in the same pot, then soften the onion and peppers, 5 minutes. Add the garlic and oregano for the last minute.
Pour in the wine and reduce by half. Add the tomatoes and rosemary, nestle the thighs back in skin-side up, skin above the sauce.
Braise uncovered in the oven 60 to 75 minutes, until the meat pulls easily from the bone and the sauce has thickened.
Fish out the bones, pull the meat into big pieces, and settle it back into the sauce. Taste for salt.
Serve over soft polenta with something red in a glass.
recipes.marketTip: September is the sweet spot — the last field peppers overlap with local mushrooms at the WNY markets. The rest of the winter, storage onions and cremini carry it fine.
recipes.pvfNote: A turkey thigh is the most underrated braising meat we sell — dark, rich, and built for tomatoes. Pastured, corn-and-soy-free birds have thighs that hold together through a long braise instead of shredding into string.

recipes.name: Sticky Oven-BBQ Turkey Wings
recipes.tagline: Chicken wings' bigger, meatier cousins — braised tender, then lacquered under the broiler for game day.

ingredients.item: turkey wings, split at the joints
rub: brown sugar, paprika, garlic powder, salt, pepper
celery sticks and ranch, if you're doing it right

instructions: Heat the oven to 325°F. Coat the wing pieces all over with the rub.
Lay them over the sliced onion in a baking dish, pour the stock around (not over) them, cover tightly with foil, and bake 90 minutes, until tender.
Uncover, pour off most of the liquid, and brush the wings generously with barbecue sauce sharpened with the vinegar.
Broil 4 to 6 minutes, brushing once more, until sticky and charred at the edges. Watch them — sugar burns fast.
Pile on a platter and serve with napkins by the roll. One turkey wing equals about four chicken wings of eating.
recipes.marketTip: This is football-season food — nothing to chase at the market except maybe celery from the last fall stands. The wings do the work.
recipes.pvfNote: Wings from turkeys that actually used them — pastured, corn-and-soy-free birds ranging the Genesee Valley — carry real meat, not just skin and hope. Braise-then-broil is the move for any working muscle.

recipes.name: The Dry-Brined Thanksgiving Turkey
recipes.tagline: Salt it Tuesday, roast it Thursday, carve it to silence. The no-drama holiday bird.

ingredients.item: kosher salt, mixed with 1 tbsp brown sugar and 1 tbsp black pepper
onions, plus 2 carrots and 2 celery stalks, roughly chopped
stock (turkey if you made it, chicken if not)

instructions: Two days out: pat the bird bone-dry and rub the salt mix everywhere — heaviest on the breast, some inside. Fridge, uncovered, on a rack. Don't rinse it later; just pat dry.

Roasting day: let the bird sit out an hour. Heat the oven to 425°F. Work the butter (mashed with chopped herbs) under the breast skin. Stuff the cavity with the lemon and a handful of herb stems — not stuffing; it slows the thighs.
Scatter the chopped vegetables in the roasting pan, set the bird on a rack above, pour in the stock.

Roast 30 minutes at 425°F, then drop to 325°F and roast about 10 to 12 minutes per pound more, until the breast reads 155°F and the thigh 175°F. Tent the breast with foil if it's browning ahead of schedule.
Rest 40 full minutes under loose foil — carve early and you pour the juice on the cutting board instead of the plate. Make gravy from the pan while you wait.
Carve in the kitchen, platter it, and take your bow.
recipes.marketTip: The November markets have the herbs, the roasting vegetables, and the last of the good storage apples for the table. Order the bird well ahead — our turkeys are seasonal and the count is fixed months before Thanksgiving.
recipes.pvfNote: A pastured, corn-and-soy-free turkey that ranged the Genesee Valley all fall doesn't need brining bags, injections, or a deep fryer to taste like something. Salt, time, and butter. The bird was the hard part, and it's already done.

recipes.name: Garlic-Rosemary Roast Leg of Lamb
recipes.tagline: The Easter table centerpiece — a half leg studded with garlic, roasted blushing pink over potatoes.

ingredients.item: rosemary, plus a small bunch of mint for serving

instructions: Salt the leg generously the night before if you can. Bring it to room temperature before roasting.
Heat the oven to 450°F. Cut small slits all over the leg and tuck a garlic sliver and a few rosemary needles into each. Rub with olive oil and pepper.
Layer the potatoes, smashed garlic, and remaining rosemary in the pan, pour in the stock, and set the lamb on top — the potatoes roast in lamb drippings, which is the second-best part of this recipe.
Roast 20 minutes at 450°F, then drop to 350°F and roast 40 to 55 minutes more, until the center reads 130°F for rosy medium-rare (grass-fed lamb is best short of medium).
Rest 20 minutes under foil. Squeeze the lemon over, shower with torn mint, and carve parallel to the bone.
Spoon the potatoes and pan juices alongside. Cold leftover lamb on toast with mustard is a private reward the next day.
recipes.marketTip: For Easter, April markets have greenhouse mint and the last great storage potatoes; in May, asparagus alongside makes it a proper spring plate.
recipes.pvfNote: Our Katahdin and White Dorper lambs stay with their mothers until weaning and finish entirely on Genesee Valley pasture — no grain, ever. Hair-sheep lamb is mild and clean, so a leg roast tastes sweet and grassy, never gamey. New batch usually lands in August; spoken for fast.

recipes.name: Lamb Burgers with Cucumber Tzatziki
recipes.tagline: Char outside, pink inside, cold garlicky tzatziki on top — the burger your cookout didn't know it was missing.

ingredients.item: tomatoes, thick-sliced, and a handful of arugula
garlic — one grated into the yogurt, one into the meat
salt, pepper, olive oil, a squeeze of lemon

instructions: Stir the tzatziki: yogurt, grated cucumber, garlic, lemon, olive oil, salt. Fridge it — it improves by the minute.
Mix the lamb gently with the cumin, oregano, garlic, a teaspoon of salt, and plenty of pepper. Shape four patties with a dimple in the middle; overworking makes them bounce.
Grill over medium-high direct heat, 3 to 4 minutes a side, to about 135°F for pink centers. Lamb fat flares — keep a cool zone and don't wander off.
Toast the buns on the grill.
Build: patty, thick slab of tomato, arugula, and an unreasonable amount of tzatziki. Eat outside while the grill's still ticking.
recipes.marketTip: Market cucumbers in July squeeze out half the water of grocery ones, which is exactly what tzatziki wants — and this is peak tomato-slab season anyway.
recipes.pvfNote: Ground lamb from our grass-fed Katahdin and White Dorper flock is the gateway lamb — mild enough for skeptics, rich enough to make beef burgers seem shy. When it's sold out between batches, that's the honest cost of a small flock.

recipes.name: Rosemary Lamb Rib Chops (Lamb Lollipops)
recipes.tagline: Two-bite chops seared hard in rosemary butter — the fanciest fifteen minutes in this whole library.

ingredients.item: flaky salt, black pepper, olive oil

instructions: Bring the chops to room temperature and season both sides well. Get a heavy skillet properly hot.
Sear the chops in a little oil, 2 minutes on the first side, untouched, until deeply browned.
Flip, drop in the butter, garlic, and rosemary, and baste the chops with a spoon for 90 seconds to 2 minutes more — about 125-130°F for rosy.
Rest 5 minutes on a warm plate, fat-side down.
Toss the greens with lemon and olive oil, lay the chops on top, and pour the rosemary butter from the pan over everything.
Serve with the bones as handles. This is finger food that thinks it's a steakhouse.
recipes.marketTip: Spring and early-fall markets have the tender greens this wants; the rosemary keeps on a windowsill all year. Nothing else to buy — spend the savings on the chops.
recipes.pvfNote: Rib chops are the rack of lamb, cut into lollipops — the most tender bite on a grass-fed Katahdin/White Dorper lamb that never ate grain. There are only sixteen per animal, which is why they come and go from the store.

recipes.name: Grilled Pork Shoulder Steaks with Charred Corn Salad
recipes.tagline: The pork steak — marbled like ribeye, priced like Tuesday — grilled over fire the way St. Louis has always known.

ingredients.item: pork shoulder steaks, about 3/4 inch thick
rub: brown sugar, paprika, garlic powder, salt, pepper
olive oil and a splash of red wine vinegar

instructions: Coat the steaks with the rub and let them sit while the grill heats — two zones, one hot, one medium.
Grill the corn and whole scallions over the hot side, turning, until charred in spots, about 6 minutes. Set aside.

Grill the steaks over medium heat 5 to 7 minutes a side. Shoulder steak has more connective tissue than a chop — take it past pink to about 165-170°F, where it turns juicy instead of chewy. Move it off flare-ups.
Rest the steaks 5 minutes. Meanwhile cut the corn off the cobs and toss with the tomatoes, sliced charred scallions, torn basil, oil, vinegar, and salt.
Serve a steak per person buried under the corn salad. Knife, fork, and no leftovers.
recipes.marketTip: Peak sweet corn and cherry tomatoes hit WNY markets together from mid-July — this salad is the whole late-summer market in one bowl.
recipes.pvfNote: Shoulder steaks are cut across the butt roast, so they carry all its marbling in a grillable slice — from Idaho Pasture Pigs raised outside on a corn-and-soy-free ration. The Midwest never let this cut go; we shouldn't have either.
